canonical_summary:
A Preliminary Safety Analysis Report (PSAR) is the safety case an applicant files with the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission to obtain a construction permit under 10 CFR 50.34(a) or, for advanced reactors, under 10 CFR 53.1309 in the Part 53 framework. For light-water reactors and most current advanced reactor construction permit applications, the PSAR is organized to the 19-chapter outline defined by Regulatory Guide 1.70, Revision 3 (November 1978) and mirrored by NUREG-0800, the Standard Review Plan. Each chapter is built section-by-section to satisfy the six-element acceptance pattern in the corresponding SRP section: Areas of Review, Acceptance Criteria, Review Procedures, Evaluation Findings, Implementation, References.

To write a PSAR for the NRC, map every PSAR section number 1-to-1 to the matching NUREG-0800 SRP section, address each Area of Review in Section I, demonstrate conformance to every item in the Section II Acceptance Criteria, and use the differences-and-alternatives table required by 10 CFR 50.34(h) or 10 CFR 52.47(a)(9) to justify any deviation from a Regulatory Guide or SRP numerical limit. Risk-informed applicants under Part 53 instead organize the PSAR around Subpart B safety criteria, the Subpart C probabilistic risk assessment and licensing basis event spectrum under 53.450, and an SSC classification (Safety-Related, NSRST, Non-Safety-Related) that flows from the PRA. Real CP-stage examples include Kairos Hermes (Docket 50-7513), Hermes 2 (Dockets 50-611 and 50-612), TerraPower Natrium (Docket 50-7508), the Long Mott Xe-100 (TICAP-structured under NEI 21-07), and Holtec SMR-300 Pioneer Units 1 and 2.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a PSAR and an FSAR?
faqs.answer: A Preliminary Safety Analysis Report (PSAR) is filed at the construction-permit stage under 10 CFR 50.34(a) (or 10 CFR 53.1309 for Part 53). It documents the preliminary design and the safety case for site suitability and bounding accident performance. A Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) is filed at the operating-license stage under 10 CFR 50.34(b). It documents the as-built or as-designed plant in final form, locks in technical specifications under Chapter 16, and incorporates the resolution of every commitment, exemption, and open item carried forward from the PSAR review and the supporting Safety Evaluation Report.

faqs.question: Do I follow Regulatory Guide 1.70 or Regulatory Guide 1.206 for the PSAR outline?
faqs.answer: Use Regulatory Guide 1.206, Revision 1 (October 2018) for any new light-water reactor construction permit or combined license application. RG 1.206 supersedes RG 1.70 for new reactors and explicitly tells applicants to organize the PSAR or FSAR to the NUREG-0800 Standard Review Plan section numbering. RG 1.70, Revision 3 (November 1978, ML011340072) remains the historical canonical 17-chapter outline; chapters 18 (Human Factors) and 19 (PRA / severe accidents) were added later through NUREG-0800. The 14 December 2021 Federal Register notice on PSAR review for LWR CPAs confirms the SRP / RG 1.206 alignment.

faqs.question: How long is a typical PSAR?
faqs.answer: Modern PSARs run 5,000 to 15,000 pages across 19 chapters plus appendices, technical reports, and topical reports incorporated by reference under Chapter 1.6. Site characterization (Chapter 2) alone is often 500 to 1,500 pages once geotechnical, seismic, hydrologic, and meteorological data are included. Chapter 15 transient and accident analyses and Chapter 19 PRA typically each exceed 1,000 pages. Advanced reactor PSARs structured under NEI 21-07 (TICAP) tend to be more compact because the safety case is organized around a finite Licensing Basis Event spectrum rather than discipline-by-discipline conformance to every SRP item.

faqs.question: What is the six-part structure of every NUREG-0800 SRP section?
faqs.answer: Every SRP section uses literal headings I through VI: I. Areas of Review, II. Acceptance Criteria (split into II.1 Requirements citing 10 CFR clauses and General Design Criteria, and II.2 SRP Acceptance Criteria citing Regulatory Guides, ASME and IEEE codes, and branch technical positions), III. Review Procedures, IV. Evaluation Findings (the boilerplate SER finding language), V. Implementation, and VI. References. The structure is identical in Chapter 2 siting sections, Chapter 6 ECCS sections, and Chapter 15 accident analysis sections. The applicant must address each Area of Review and demonstrate conformance to each Acceptance Criterion.

faqs.question: What is a differences and alternatives table and when do I need one?
faqs.answer: Where a design does not meet an SRP item literally, 10 CFR 50.34(h) (for Part 50) and 10 CFR 52.47(a)(9) (for Part 52) require the applicant to identify the deviation and justify an alternative method of meeting the underlying regulation. The expected artifact is a differences-and-alternatives table that lists each SRP section, the specific SRP acceptance criterion, the design feature that does not meet it, and the technical basis for the alternative. This table is what NRC reviewers and Advisory Committee on Reactor Safeguards (ACRS) panels go to first; missing or thin entries are a leading driver of Requests for Additional Information.

faqs.question: How does a PSAR change under 10 CFR Part 53?
faqs.answer: The Part 53 final rule (91 FR, FR Doc. 2026-06048, effective April 29, 2026) does not require the legacy 19-chapter outline. PSAR contents sit in Subpart H around 10 CFR 53.1309, organized around Subpart B safety criteria (licensing basis events, frequency-consequence target, quantitative health objectives) and Subpart C design requirements. 10 CFR 53.450 anchors PRA or systematic risk evaluation use, and SSC classification (Safety-Related, NSRST, Non-Safety-Related) flows from the PRA. 10 CFR 53.440(k) adds a new chemical hazards assessment relevant to molten salt and sodium designs. The PRA chapter becomes the organizing spine rather than a Chapter 19 appendix.

faqs.question: What are the most common NRC Request for Additional Information (RAI) deficiencies?
faqs.answer: Recurring deficiency categories across NuScale, Kairos, X-energy, Holtec, TerraPower, and Oklo dockets are: (1) incomplete accident analysis methodology and mechanistic source term, (2) thin SSC safety classification and Quality Assurance program implementation, the basis NRC cited in the Oklo Aurora denial (NRC press release 22-002, January 6, 2022), (3) gaps in site characterization and geotechnical data including boreholes near the reactor building footprint, (4) digital instrumentation and controls with human factors engineering, (5) PRA scope and quality against ASME/ANS RA-S-1.4, (6) fuel qualification and graphite or structural materials irradiation data, and (7) aircraft impact assessment under 10 CFR 50.150.

faqs.question: Which advanced reactor PSARs are public and worth studying?
faqs.answer: The Kairos Hermes PSAR (Docket 50-7513, ML21272A375) is the cleanest public hybrid of NUREG-1537 and Licensing Modernization Project framing for a non-power test reactor. Hermes 2 (Dockets 50-611 and 50-612, ML23195A124) is the two-unit extension. TerraPower Natrium (Docket 50-7508, supported by NATD-LIC-RPRT-0002-A Principal Design Criteria at ML24283A066) demonstrates LMP under RG 1.233 paired with conventional power-reactor SAR organization. Long Mott Energy Xe-100 (application package ML25090A057) is the first major application built on the NEI 21-07 TICAP chapter structure endorsed by RG 1.253. Holtec SMR-300 Pioneer Units 1 and 2 (Docket ID NRC-2026-0265) follows traditional NUREG-0800 chapters as a light-water design.

faqs.question: How does Chapter 19 PRA differ from a Part 53 risk-informed PSAR?
faqs.answer: Under the legacy 19-chapter format, the PRA appears in Chapter 19 as a supporting study against the targets in NUREG-0800 SRP 19.0, Revision 3 (March 2015, ML15089A068) and the ASME/ANS RA-Sa standard endorsed by Regulatory Guide 1.200. Core damage frequency and large early release frequency are calibrated against design-basis accident analyses in Chapter 15. Under Part 53, the PRA or systematic risk evaluation under 10 CFR 53.450 is the organizing spine. Every SSC classification, every special treatment requirement, and the defense-in-depth assessment trace back to the licensing basis event spectrum and the frequency-consequence target inherited from the Licensing Modernization Project (NEI 18-04 endorsed by RG 1.233).

faqs.question: What is the role of NEI 21-07 (TICAP) in writing a PSAR?
faqs.answer: NEI 21-07, Technology-Inclusive Content of Application Project (TICAP), Revision 1, endorsed by Regulatory Guide 1.253, defines an eight-chapter SAR for non-light-water reactors: (1) general plant information, (2) methodologies and site information, (3) Licensing Basis Event analysis anchored in NEI 18-04, (4) integrated evaluations including defense-in-depth, (5) safety functions and SSC safety classification, (6) Safety-Related SSC criteria, (7) Non-Safety-Related with Special Treatment SSC criteria, (8) plant programs. ARCAP adds chapters 9 through 12 covering human factors, operational programs, technical specifications, and Quality Assurance with ITAAC. The Xe-100 Long Mott application (ML25090A057) is the leading public example of this structure.

faqs.question: Where do AI tools actually help when drafting a PSAR?
faqs.answer: AI tools are most useful for cross-mapping the PSAR table of contents to NUREG-0800 SRP sections, building the differences-and-alternatives table required by 10 CFR 50.34(h) or 10 CFR 52.47(a)(9), tracing each design commitment to the specific GDC in 10 CFR 50 Appendix A or the Subpart B criterion in Part 53, drafting Chapter 1.8 Regulatory Guide conformance tables, mining historical NuScale, AP1000, and Hermes RAIs to anticipate likely staff questions, and consistency-checking section numbering against the SRP six-part structure. They are not a substitute for the licensed engineering analyses that ground Chapters 3, 6, 15, and 19, nor for the QA program audits that NRC will perform under 10 CFR 50 Appendix B.

faqs.question: What is the typical PSAR review timeline?
faqs.answer: Recent advanced reactor construction permit reviews have ranged from approximately 18 to 30 months from docketing to permit issuance. Kairos Hermes was docketed November 29, 2021 and the permit issued December 12, 2023 (about 25 months). Hermes 2 was filed July 14, 2023 and permits issued November 21, 2024 (about 16 months for the two-unit follow-on). TerraPower Natrium was docketed May 21, 2024 and the construction permit issued March 4, 2026 (about 22 months). The X-energy Xe-100 Long Mott application is on an 18-month review schedule. Holtec SMR-300 uses a phased CP, with Part 1 accepted in February 2026 and Part 2 due by mid-2027.

h1: How to write a PSAR for the NRC
meta_title: How to write a PSAR for the NRC
meta_description: A reference for writing a Preliminary Safety Analysis Report to NUREG-0800 and Regulatory Guide 1.70, with Part 53, RAI patterns, and advanced reactor examples.

sections.heading: What a PSAR is and why it exists
paragraphs: A Preliminary Safety Analysis Report (PSAR) is the document an applicant submits to the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission to obtain a construction permit. Under 10 CFR 50.34(a) it is the safety case for a Part 50 construction permit; under 10 CFR 52.79 it is the safety case inside a Part 52 combined license application; under 10 CFR 53.1309 it is the safety case for a construction permit issued through the Part 53 framework finalized at 91 FR (FR Doc. 2026-06048), effective April 29, 2026. The PSAR is what the NRC staff reads to determine whether the proposed plant, at the proposed site, can be built and operated without undue risk to public health and safety.

Where the Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) documents the as-designed plant under 10 CFR 50.34(b), the PSAR is preliminary. The design need not be complete, but the bounding accident analyses, the site characterization, and the regulatory commitments must be sufficient for the NRC to make a reasonable assurance finding. The reviewer's companion document is NUREG-0800, the Standard Review Plan, which mirrors the PSAR section-by-section.

The 14 December 2021 Federal Register notice on safety review of light-water power-reactor construction permit applications confirms that the staff expects PSAR submissions for new reactors to be organized to the NUREG-0800 / Regulatory Guide 1.206 outline. That alignment is the contract between applicant and reviewer.
bullets: 10 CFR 50.34(a) governs PSAR content for Part 50 construction permits
10 CFR 52.79 governs the FSAR-equivalent inside Part 52 combined license applications
10 CFR 53.1309 governs PSAR content under Part 53 (effective April 29, 2026)
NUREG-0800 is the reviewer's companion to the PSAR and dictates the outline

sections.heading: The 19-chapter Regulatory Guide 1.70 framework
paragraphs: Regulatory Guide 1.70, Revision 3 (November 1978, ML011340072) defined a 17-chapter Safety Analysis Report outline for light-water reactors. NUREG-0800 added Chapter 18 (Human Factors Engineering) and Chapter 19 (Probabilistic Risk Assessment and Severe Accident Evaluation), producing the 19-chapter format used by every current LWR PSAR or FSAR. Regulatory Guide 1.206, Revision 1 (October 2018) is the bridging guide that tells new-reactor applicants to organize the PSAR to the NUREG-0800 section numbering.

subsections.heading: Chapters 1 through 10
bullets: Chapter 1: Introduction and General Description of the Plant. Sub-sections 1.1 through 1.9 including 1.8 Conformance with Regulatory Guides and 1.9 Resolution of Unresolved Safety Issues.

Chapter 2: Site Characteristics. Sections 2.1 Geography and Demography, 2.2 Nearby Industrial / Transportation / Military Facilities, 2.3 Meteorology, 2.4 Hydrologic Engineering, 2.5 Geology, Seismology, and Geotechnical Engineering.

Chapter 3: Design of Structures, Systems, Components, and Equipment. 3.1 Conformance with 10 CFR 50 Appendix A General Design Criteria, 3.2 SSC Classification, 3.3 Wind and Tornado, 3.4 Flood, 3.5 Missile Protection, 3.6 Pipe Break, 3.7 Seismic Design, 3.8 Category I Structures, 3.9 Mechanical Systems and Components, 3.10 Seismic and Dynamic Qualification, 3.11 Environmental Qualification.
Chapter 4: Reactor. Fuel system, nuclear design, thermal-hydraulic design, reactor materials, reactivity control.
Chapter 5: Reactor Coolant System and Connected Systems. RCPB integrity, reactor vessel, pumps, steam generators, pressurizer, residual heat removal.
Chapter 6: Engineered Safety Features. 6.1 ESF Materials, 6.2 Containment, 6.3 ECCS, 6.4 Habitability, 6.5 Fission Product Removal.
Chapter 7: Instrumentation and Controls. Reactor trip system, ESF actuation, safety-related display, diverse I&C, data communications.
Chapter 8: Electric Power. Offsite power and onsite AC (including emergency diesel generators) and DC.
Chapter 9: Auxiliary Systems. Fuel storage and handling, water systems, process auxiliaries, HVAC, fire protection.
Chapter 10: Steam and Power Conversion. Turbine-generator, main steam, condensate, feedwater, auxiliary feedwater.

subsections.heading: Chapters 11 through 19
bullets: Chapter 11: Radioactive Waste Management. Source terms, liquid, gaseous, and solid waste systems, effluent monitoring.
Chapter 12: Radiation Protection. ALARA, sources, shielding, dose assessment, health physics program.
Chapter 13: Conduct of Operations. Organization, training, emergency planning, procedures, physical security, fitness for duty.
Chapter 14: Initial Test Program and ITAAC. Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance Criteria for design certification and combined license stages.
Chapter 15: Transient and Accident Analyses. 15.1 through 15.8 covering the eight initiator categories from heat removal increases through ATWS.
Chapter 16: Technical Specifications. Generic and plant-specific tech specs aligned with NUREG-1430 through 1434 templates.
Chapter 17: Quality Assurance. 17.1 Design and Construction QA, 17.2 Operations QA, 17.3 Design Reliability Assurance Program, 17.4 Maintenance Rule.
Chapter 18: Human Factors Engineering. Built to NUREG-0711 program elements from operating experience review through human performance monitoring.

Chapter 19: Probabilistic Risk Assessment and Severe Accident Evaluation. Internal events, internal and external hazards, all modes, Levels 1 and 2 (and Level 3 where applicable), SAMA / SAMDA evaluation.

sections.heading: Using NUREG-0800 as your outline
paragraphs: NUREG-0800 was issued as NUREG-75/087 in 1975, renumbered in 1980, and most recently consolidated in March 2007 and May 2010 with section-level revisions through 2018. The SRP is not a regulation. The binding rules are 10 CFR 50.34, 10 CFR Part 52, 10 CFR Part 100, and the General Design Criteria at 10 CFR 50 Appendix A. The SRP tells the staff how to judge compliance with those rules, and every SRP section has the same six-part structure.

An applicant builds the PSAR by mapping each section number 1-to-1 to the matching SRP section. The PSAR table of contents is isomorphic to the SRP table of contents. For each section the applicant addresses every Area of Review listed under Section I, demonstrates conformance with every item under Section II Acceptance Criteria, and provides the analyses, drawings, and commitments that Section III Review Procedures tell the reviewer to audit. The Safety Evaluation Report (SER) the staff issues then tracks the same section numbers and uses the Section IV Evaluation Findings boilerplate.

subsections.heading: The six elements every SRP section contains
bullets: I. Areas of Review: systems, parameters, interfaces, inputs the reviewer must see.

II. Acceptance Criteria: (II.1) Requirements (specific 10 CFR clauses, GDCs, Part 100 siting rules) and (II.2) SRP Acceptance Criteria citing Regulatory Guides, ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code Section III, IEEE 603, ANSI/ANS standards, branch technical positions.
III. Review Procedures: the step-by-step audit and confirmatory calculation method.
IV. Evaluation Findings: the SER boilerplate finding language used if the section is accepted.
V. Implementation: effective date and applicability of the SRP revision.
VI. References.

subsections.heading: Concrete section examples to anchor your draft
bullets: SRP 2.5.4 (Stability of Subsurface Materials and Foundations, Rev. 3, March 2007) cites GDC 2 and 10 CFR 100.23 as requirements; accepts Regulatory Guide 1.132 site investigations and RG 1.198 liquefaction methods.
SRP 6.2.1 (Containment Functional Design) cites GDC 16, 38, 50 and accepts ASME containment code margins plus pressure-temperature analyses using NRC-accepted codes such as CONTAIN and GOTHIC.

SRP 15.0.3 governs the transient and accident analysis approach; the numerical acceptance limits (DNBR, the 2200 F peak clad temperature under 10 CFR 50.46, dose limits under 10 CFR 50.34(a)(1) and Part 100) come directly from regulation.
SRP 19.0 Rev. 3 (March 2015, ML15089A068) sets PRA scope and acceptance criteria for severe accident evaluation, tied to the ASME/ANS RA-Sa PRA standard endorsed by Regulatory Guide 1.200.

sections.heading: How to write each chapter: lengths, supporting analyses, common pitfalls
paragraphs: A modern PSAR runs 5,000 to 15,000 pages, with the bulk concentrated in Chapter 2 (site characterization), Chapter 3 (SSC design), Chapter 15 (accident analyses), and Chapter 19 (PRA). The right way to write each chapter is to start with the corresponding SRP section, populate the differences-and-alternatives table required by 10 CFR 50.34(h) and 10 CFR 52.47(a)(9) early, and let the supporting analyses be drafted against that scaffolding.

subsections.heading: Chapter 2 (site characteristics)
bullets: Typical length 500 to 1,500 pages.

Supporting analyses: probabilistic seismic hazard analysis to RG 1.208; geotechnical investigation to RG 1.132; liquefaction screening to RG 1.198; flood hazard reevaluation; meteorological tower data with chi/Q calculations; population distribution to 50 miles.

Common pitfalls: thin borehole coverage at the reactor building footprint (the Hermes audit cited this exact gap, NRC audit plan ML23130A183), inconsistent ground motion response spectrum between Chapter 2.5 and Chapter 3.7, and outdated population data.

subsections.heading: Chapter 3 (design of SSCs)
bullets: Typical length 800 to 2,000 pages.

Supporting analyses: General Design Criteria conformance table for all 64 GDCs in 10 CFR 50 Appendix A (or the Advanced Reactor Design Criteria per RG 1.232 for non-LWRs), seismic Category I qualification, equipment environmental qualification to 10 CFR 50.49.

Common pitfalls: GDC conformance statements that read as commitments without supporting design evidence, missing pipe break analyses under SRP 3.6, and inconsistent classification between 3.2 and the Chapter 17.3 D-RAP list.

subsections.heading: Chapter 6 (engineered safety features) and Chapter 15 (transient and accident analyses)
bullets: Typical combined length 1,500 to 3,500 pages.

Supporting analyses: ECCS performance to 10 CFR 50.46 acceptance criteria, containment pressure-temperature, dose calculations to Part 100 and 10 CFR 50.34(a)(1), the eight 15.x event categories from increased secondary heat removal through ATWS.

Common pitfalls: incomplete spectrum of initiating events, undocumented code qualification (the same pattern that drove multi-round Xe-100 RAIs on the Transient and Safety Analysis Methodology topical at ML24121A288 and ML25077A285), and unconservative single-failure assumptions.

subsections.heading: Chapter 17 (Quality Assurance) and Chapter 19 (PRA)
bullets: Typical Chapter 17 length 200 to 500 pages; Chapter 19 typically exceeds 1,000 pages with the supporting PRA model documented in topical reports incorporated by reference.

Supporting analyses: 10 CFR 50 Appendix B QA program description, Design Reliability Assurance Program SSC list, full-power internal events PRA, low-power and shutdown PRA, internal fire, internal flood, seismic, and other external hazard PRAs to RG 1.200 capability category targets.

Common pitfalls: QA program description disconnected from procurement and software QA for digital I&C, PRA scope gaps that the Final Safety Evaluation for NuScale Chapter 19 (closed July 17, 2020) only resolved after iterative RAI rounds across every hazard category.

sections.heading: PSAR under 10 CFR Part 53 vs 10 CFR Part 50
paragraphs: The Part 53 final rule was published at 91 FR (FR Doc. 2026-06048) on March 30, 2026 and is effective April 29, 2026. It codifies an optional pathway in parallel with Part 50 and Part 52. The Subpart architecture relevant to a PSAR is: Subpart B (Safety Criteria, roughly 10 CFR 53.200 through 53.290) sets technology-neutral performance thresholds anchored in the Licensing Modernization Project frequency-consequence target. Subpart C (Design and Analysis Requirements, roughly 10 CFR 53.400 through 53.490) mandates a probabilistic risk assessment or systematic risk evaluation (or combination) at 10 CFR 53.450 to identify licensing basis events, classify SSCs as Safety-Related, Non-Safety-Related with Special Treatment (NSRST), or Non-Safety-Related, and demonstrate that Subpart B is met. 10 CFR 53.440(k) adds a chemical hazards assessment that is novel relative to Part 50. Subpart H consolidates the licensing pathways including the construction permit content at 10 CFR 53.1309.

In a traditional RG 1.70 PSAR, Chapters 6, 15, and 3 carry most of the safety argument and the PRA appears in Chapter 19 as a supporting study. Under Part 53 the analytic center of gravity shifts: the PRA / SRE and the licensing basis event chapter become the spine, every SSC classification and special-treatment requirement trace back to it, and deterministic design-basis accident analysis contracts to confirming that the worst events in the high-consequence region of the F-C curve stay inside Subpart B thresholds. Industry guidance for the new structure is NEI 21-07 (TICAP), Revision 1, endorsed by Regulatory Guide 1.253, which defines an eight-chapter SAR; ARCAP adds chapters 9 through 12 for human factors, operational programs, technical specifications, and Quality Assurance with ITAAC.
bullets: Subpart B sets the safety thresholds; Subpart C requires the PRA / SRE; Subpart H sets PSAR contents around 10 CFR 53.1309
NEI 21-07 Revision 1 defines the eight-chapter TICAP SAR, endorsed by Regulatory Guide 1.253
ARCAP guidance covers chapters 9 through 12 plus environmental, security, and emergency planning content outside the SAR
Kairos Hermes and TerraPower Natrium were filed under Part 50 but used Licensing Modernization Project framing that prefigures the Part 53 structure

sections.heading: Common RAI deficiency patterns
paragraphs: The NRC's 2013 New Reactor Licensing Process Lessons Learned Review (ML13059A239) concluded that Request for Additional Information volume is driven by late identification of issues and inconsistent management of disagreements. Across NuScale, Kairos, X-energy, Holtec, TerraPower, and Oklo dockets the recurring categories are consistent.
bullets: Methodology gaps in accident analysis and mechanistic source term. The Oklo Aurora COLA was denied without prejudice on January 6, 2022 (NRC press release 22-002) because Oklo repeatedly failed to provide substantive information on the maximum credible accident analysis; multiple Topical Report revisions (ML20305A587, ML21184A002, ML21278B098) did not resolve it.

SSC safety classification and Quality Assurance program implementation. The same Oklo denial named QA program implementation as a primary deficiency. NuScale topical reports on NSRST classification drew the same pattern.

Steam generator, thermal-hydraulic, and integral-effects testing. NuScale US600 closed in 2022 with three unresolved Tier 2 open items carried into the design certification rule at 10 CFR Part 52 Appendix G (Federal Register January 19, 2023), including helical-coil steam generator tube integrity under density-wave oscillations.
Site characterization and geotechnical data. The Hermes audit (ML23130A183) cited the absence of boreholes in the close vicinity of the reactor building footprint.

Digital instrumentation and controls and human factors. The AP1000 Amendment Revision 19 final rule (Federal Register December 30, 2011) required converting open Design Acceptance Criteria in Chapters 7 and 18 to specific design information.
Probabilistic Risk Assessment scope, quality, and conformance to ASME/ANS RA-S-1.4 endorsed by Regulatory Guide 1.200.

Fuel qualification and graphite or structural materials irradiation data. The Xe-100 Graphite Core Components topical (ML24309A123) and Reactor Core Design Methods topical (ML24067A310, ML25262A126) absorbed multi-round RAIs.
Aircraft impact assessment under 10 CFR 50.150 and emergency planning zone sizing for SMRs.

sections.heading: Real examples: Kairos, X-energy, Natrium, Holtec
paragraphs: The best public PSARs to study sit in ADAMS under four dockets. Each illustrates a different structural choice for the same underlying regulatory frame.

subsections.heading: Kairos Power Hermes (Docket 50-7513) and Hermes 2 (Dockets 50-611 and 50-612)
bullets: Hermes 1 PSAR package ML21272A375; application filed September 29, 2021; docketed November 29, 2021; construction permit issued December 12, 2023; Final SER cover ML22143B016.
Hermes 2 PSAR ML23195A124; application filed July 14, 2023; two construction permits issued November 21, 2024; package ML23195A121.
Structure: hybrid layout closer to NUREG-1537 (non-power reactor guide) supplemented with LMP elements. Hermes is a test reactor, not a power reactor.

subsections.heading: TerraPower Natrium / Kemmerer Unit 1 (Docket 50-7508)
bullets: Application filed March 28, 2024; supplements May 2 and May 9, 2024; docketed May 21, 2024.
Final SER issued December 1, 2025; construction permit issued March 4, 2026 (the first NRC CP for a commercial non-LWR).
Principal Design Criteria topical NATD-LIC-RPRT-0002-A: cover ML24101A362, content ML24283A066, final SE ML25329A252; mechanistic source term topical ML24017A116; readiness assessment ML24060A227.

Structure: LMP / RIPB under NEI 18-04 Rev. 1 endorsed by Regulatory Guide 1.233, with Natrium-specific Principal Design Criteria derived from Regulatory Guide 1.232. Pairs LMP with conventional power-reactor SAR organization.

subsections.heading: Long Mott Energy (X-energy Xe-100) at Seadrift, TX
bullets: Application filed March 31, 2025 by Long Mott Energy, LLC; package ML25090A057.
Four-unit Xe-100 HTGR plant (4 x 80 MWe = 320 MWe / 800 MWth); docketed May 2025 on an 18-month review schedule.
Draft PSAR readiness assessment ML26045A139; earlier readiness assessment plan ML24250A186.
Structure: first major application built explicitly on the NEI 21-07 Revision 1 TICAP eight-chapter structure endorsed with clarifications by Regulatory Guide 1.253.

subsections.heading: Holtec SMR, LLC Pioneer Units 1 and 2 at Palisades
bullets: Part 1 of a phased CP submitted December 31, 2025; includes PSAR, Limited Work Authorization, exemption requests, and Environmental Report.
Federal Register acceptance notice published February 27, 2026 (91 FR 9892); Docket ID NRC-2026-0265; Part 2 due no later than mid-2027.
Structure: light-water; tracks NUREG-0800 chapters closely, with phased CP exemptions analogous to historic Part 50 construction permits.

sections.heading: Where AI agents help (and where they don't)
paragraphs: A PSAR is a long, structured document with stable inter-section dependencies and a fixed external scaffolding (NUREG-0800, the General Design Criteria, the Regulatory Guides, the ASME and IEEE codes, the Federal Register history). That is the shape of work where AI agents return real leverage today.

subsections.heading: What AI agents are good at
bullets: Cross-mapping the PSAR table of contents to NUREG-0800 SRP section numbers and flagging gaps before the staff acceptance review does.
Building the differences-and-alternatives table required by 10 CFR 50.34(h) and 10 CFR 52.47(a)(9) by parsing the design and the SRP Section II acceptance criteria together.
Drafting Chapter 1.8 Regulatory Guide conformance tables and Chapter 17.3 D-RAP SSC lists.
Tracing every design commitment to a specific GDC in 10 CFR 50 Appendix A or a Subpart B criterion in Part 53.
Mining historical NuScale, AP1000, Hermes, Natrium, and Xe-100 RAIs to anticipate likely staff questions on a given chapter.
Consistency-checking section numbering, internal cross-references, acronyms, and the six-part SRP structure across thousands of pages.

subsections.heading: What AI agents do not replace
bullets: The licensed engineering analyses that ground Chapter 3 (seismic, structural, equipment qualification), Chapter 6 (ECCS, containment), Chapter 15 (transient and accident analyses), and Chapter 19 (PRA).
QA program audits conducted under 10 CFR 50 Appendix B.
Site investigation field work (boreholes, meteorological towers, hydrologic measurements) required by Regulatory Guides 1.132, 1.208, and 1.198.
ACRS subcommittee and full-committee engagements, which require a human responsible licensed engineer of record.
